data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: 2 High Yield Income Alternatives To Shares
data.description:
Introduction

Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (NYSE:HPE) is a diversified technology company that has long specialized in servers and computer hardware but has recently expanded into services such as cloud computing. The AI revolution has also affected Hewlett Packard Enterprise’s business model. Earlier this year, the company issued 27 million mandatory convertible shares with a dividend of 7.625% at par value. The shares would convert to 3.1056 common shares on September 1, 2027. After investigating the company’s financial health and income offerings, I believe investors would be best served by selling cash-secured put options to go long on Hewlett Packard Enterprise shares.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise Financial Results

HPE has experienced revenue headwinds this year. Through the first nine months of its fiscal year 2024, HPE has seen total revenue decline by more than $100 million (less than 1%) driven by a $400 million decline in product revenue. Services revenue increased by more than $240 million compared to a year ago and helped offset the decline in product revenue. Despite the decline in product sales, product costs went up by $50 million, but the cost of services increased by less than service revenue growth, which helped control total costs. Earnings from operations ended up declining by $85 million to just under $1.5 billion year to date.

HPE’s sales decline was led by a decline in Intelligent Edge sales. The drop also led to a decline in the operating margin. Intelligent Edge is a combination of products and services used to help organizations analyze data. Management has been quick to point out that the decline in Intelligent Edge comes off pandemic-related highs and that the segment is making sequential sales improvements and should grow from current levels.

HPE’s balance sheet consists of over $3.6 billion in cash and $7.6 billion in inventory to round out more than $22 billion in current assets. Goodwill highlights more than $18 billion of intangible assets. HPE has nearly $12 billion in long-term financing receivables and $5.7 billion in property, plant, and equipment. HPE carries a large vendor payable liability, which grew by nearly $3 billion to over $10 billion during the fiscal year. HPE also decreased its debt by $550 million (including notes payable and short-term borrowings). Shareholder equity dropped slightly to $22.1 billion.

Cash Flow Generation Supports Preferred Dividends

The new preferred dividends will create a dividend obligation of $103 million per year. HPE is generating the cash flow required to support these dividends with some caveats that investors should be aware of. HPE has engaged in a massive inventory buildup this year which has been financed by accounts payable growth. At some point, these vendor obligations will need to be repaid and become a cash flow headwind. Nonetheless, operating cash flow in the first three fiscal quarters grew by more than $700 million to over $2.3 billion. I’m hopeful that the inventory build will translate into sales quickly against the company’s AI backlog.

After capital expenditures of $1.75 billion, HPE generated a little more than $550 million in free cash flow so far in 2024. By including the preferred share obligations, HPE is on par with creating the free cash flow to cover both common and preferred share dividends. HPE used cash on hand to lower its debt by more than $600 million this year.

Mandatory Convertible Preferred vs. Put Options

The convertible preferred shares have already rallied to over $60 per share since they were issued a month ago. Despite the surge in pricing, the purchase of these shares is still of better value than buying common shares. This is because at the mandatory conversion rate upon maturity, HPE’s preferred shares are trading at a common share value of $19.50, which is a 5.7% discount to common shares. Additionally, the $3.8125 per share dividend (6.3% dividend yield) adds more value to the preferred shares.

With respect to options, investors can find even more value by selling the $19 strike option expiring on November 22 for $0.20 or $20 per contract. The income generated from this transaction would generate an annualized yield of over 10.3%. Additionally, if assigned at $19 per share, investors would be acquiring shares at an 8.1% discount to Friday’s share price (not including the offset of the premium from selling options). The options path provides the best income and share price discount.

Risks to HPE

HPE is a large company that manufactures complex products. These products require a great degree of international trade, especially from China. Should that relationship be disrupted by tariffs or any other geopolitical instability, investors can expect that to have a negative effect on HPE’s production and consequently, its sales. The AI boom fizzling out would have a negative effect as well since the company is dedicating more and more of its resources towards meeting the demands of that subsector. As it currently stands, analysts are moderately optimistic about HPE’s future. Analysts have HPE’s earnings growing modestly to $2.22 per share in 2026, placing shares at 9.3 times those levels.

Conclusion

Hewlett Packard Enterprise continues to diversify its product and service offerings to grow sales. The company is poised to grow earnings by meeting the AI needs of its customers. While the mandatory convertible preferred shares offer a better discount with a higher dividend compared to the common shares, cash-secured put options offer an even higher discount and income, which is where I’m advising investors to steer their capital.

data.title: When J.B. Hunt Wins, Everyone Wins (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I wasn't planning on writing this article, to be completely honest.

However, J.B. Hunt Transport Services (NASDAQ:JBHT) just reported fantastic earnings. Although its 3Q24 numbers were nothing to write home about, the giant reported a good outlook, as it sees fundamental improvements in the areas it serves.

This is a big deal, as J.B. Hunt is one of the biggest transportation companies in America. Although its market cap is “just” $17 billion, it is one of the biggest players in the intermodal business. Half of its revenue is generated in intermodal. In this segment, it has the largest 52' high-cube container fleet and the largest drayage fleet in North America.

I believe most readers will be familiar with J.B. Hunt intermodal containers on Class I railroads. It often looks something like this:

To use the company's own words:

Throughout the years that followed, JBI established multiple agreements with other Class I railroads. JBI draws on the intermodal services of these rail carriers for the underlying linehaul movement of its equipment between rail ramps. The origin and destination pickup and delivery services (drayage) are handled by our company-owned tractors for the majority of our intermodal loads, while third-party dray carriers are used where economical. - JBHT 2023 10-K.

Meanwhile, more than 40% of its revenues come from Dedicated Contract Services, which are higher-margin operations focused on fleet creation, conversion, and implementation of supply chain solutions for customers. In other words, these are long-term deals (5-year average) that help customers manage their transportation needs.

It also makes money from service-focused Integrated Capacity Solutions, Final Mile Services, and Truckload operations. However, these are just minor operations compared to its intermodal and contract services segments.

With that said, my portfolio does not include the JBHT ticker, as I opted for railroads and less-than-truckload companies that enjoy wider moats.

Nonetheless, covering JBHT has always been important to me, as the company tells us so much about the U.S. economy — especially about the companies it serves.

Hence, my most recent article on April 18 was titled “The Coal Mine Canary - Don't Ignore J.B. Hunt Earnings.”

In this article, I'll discuss its 3Q24 earnings and explain what this means for the company, the customers it serves, and the economy.

So, let's get to it!

Bad Results

As I already briefly mentioned, the 3Q24 numbers were not that spectacular, as the company saw 3% lower revenues and 7% lower operating income. This lowered diluted earnings per share by 17%.

As we can see below, revenues were down across the board.

This makes sense, as leading economic indicators have hinted at weakness, including the average of the New York and Philadelphia Fed current shipments index (part of their monthly manufacturing surveys). Since early 2023, optimism has been gone completely.

Moreover, as some readers may recall, this is supported by a contraction in the ISM Manufacturing Index, which is another good leading indicator that has been struggling since early 2023.

The ISM Index peaked in mid-2021. That's when manufacturing sentiment peaked in the United States, followed by elevated inflation, aggressive rate hikes, and geopolitical uncertainty.

As such, it may not come as a surprise that JBHT shares have not gone anywhere since mid-2021. That's the power of leading indicators.

Going back to JBHT's results, the company noted that despite positive volume trends, yield pressures continue to impact its margins, making it important to focus on productivity improvement without hurting future growth potential.

For example, in intermodal, the company saw 0% revenue growth and 13% lower operating income, a clear sign of margin pressure.

The same happened in Dedicated Contract Services, where 5% lower revenues caused a 7% decline in operating income. Revenue per truck per week fell to $5,073.

Now, it's time for good news.

Light At The End Of The Tunnel

If we set aside pressure on yield, we see that the company reported year-over-year volume growth of 5% in the third quarter. According to the company, this outperformed normal seasonal trends.

Moreover, the company's consolidated volumes showed consistent monthly growth:

  • 7% in July.
  • 4% in August.
  • 4% in September.

JBHT is also winning back lost business due to improving service levels.

While pricing remains a key lever for enhancing margins, J.B. Hunt is optimistic about the trends in bid compliance from customers. According to the company, this could lead to better efficiencies and help balance lane imbalances in its operations. In fact, the company reached the highest service levels since it introduced the J.B. Hunt 360 Box in 2019.

This is a flexible service for companies in need of transportation capacities, as it connects trailers to available carriers. The more members it has, the bigger the advantages.

It has recently implemented pricing adjustments and feels very confident in its ability to remain competitive due to strong service levels and the ability to meet surging demand. In general, I noticed that many intermodal-focused companies have cut expenses while keeping an eye on excess volumes to support growth without having to accelerate spending.

Moreover, during its earnings call, the company noted that current truckload pricing is unsustainable. This is causing shippers to move freight back to the rails. Note that intermodal rail heavily competes with truckload companies, as it's one of the few transportation types that can be shipped by trucks rather easily.

This bodes very well for Class I railroads.

It also helps that inflation has created demand for more efficient supply chains. This benefits JBHT due to its service-focused business model. Especially in an event of rising cyclical economic growth, this could bode very well for both volumes and yield.

Lastly, as we sit here today, capacity is not a top concern for customers, but they are aware that the current market dynamics will shift, though the timing and rate of change remains uncertain. Customers are also aware that there has been significant cost inflation across our industry and margins are under pressure. With these knowns, we will continue to work with them to help drive efficiencies across their supply chain while balancing our need to repair our margins. - JBHT 3Q24 Earnings Call.

In other words, so far, the company is seeing positive demand developments. However, margins remain an issue, as it is investing to improve network fluidity — especially in Southern California. So far, these efforts are paying off and could pave the way for both higher volumes and better pricing.

I see more evidence today that we can scale our investments in our people, technology and capacity to capture additional market share at better returns as the freight cycle inflection occurs. I remain confident in our strategy and maintain our focus on generating appropriate returns on capital to deliver value for our shareholders. - JBHT 3Q24 Earnings Call.

As one can imagine, this is good for shareholders.

Valuation

As a result of JBHT's positive comments, the entire transportation sector got an uplift, beating the S&P 500 by a substantial margin — relatively speaking.

This makes sense, as the market has eagerly waited for a reason to jump back into these cyclical companies.

Looking at the FactSet data below, JBHT is expected to see a 19% EPS contraction this year, pressured by the aforementioned headwinds from weak volumes and yield.

However, going forward, analysts expect 25% and 24% EPS growth in 2025 and 2026, respectively.

Applying the company's long-term P/E average of 23.1x results in a fair stock price of $203, 16% above the current price. This is based on 2026 EPS expectations of $8.80. Six months ago, that number was roughly $10.00. This shows how expectations have been lowered recently, as analysts were not willing to incorporate a potential demand bottom in their research.

While I cannot blame them, I expect upgrades in the upcoming months if leading transportation indicators support the company's comments. In that scenario, I expect the stock to surge well beyond $230 instead of just $203.

The only reason why I am not buying JBHT is because of my exposure to railroads. I prefer these investments over JBHT and its peers. Given JBHT's good results, I expect that all of my Class I railroad holdings will confirm intermodal strength in the weeks ahead. If they also comment on pricing tailwinds, I'm very upbeat about the future of the transportation industry.

Takeaway

J.B. Hunt's 3Q24 earnings reveal a mix of challenges and optimism.

While the company's numbers were pressured by lower revenues and margins, there are clear signs of a recovery on the horizon, especially in its Intermodal and Dedicated Contract Services segments.

The 5% volume growth and improved service levels are very encouraging, indicating that J.B. Hunt is regaining lost business and benefiting from shifting freight trends.

Although I'm not adding JBHT to my portfolio due to my preference for railroads, I'm optimistic about the future of transportation, especially as cyclical growth returns and pricing power improve across the sector.

It's too early to say we're out of the woods, but these developments are very encouraging!

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Volume Growth: J.B. Hunt reported 5% year-over-year volume growth, outperforming seasonal trends.
  • Service Improvements: The company has hit the highest service levels since 2019, which should help drive efficiencies and customer retention.
  • Pricing Power Potential: With bid compliance improving, J.B. Hunt could see better pricing.
  • Freight Shift: The move from truckload back to rail intermodal positions J.B. Hunt to capture additional market share.

Cons:

  • Margin Pressure: Yield pressures are hurting margins despite improving volumes. This is a key challenge for the company.
  • Weak Truckload Pricing: Truckload pricing remains unsustainable, which could weigh on the company's truckload profitability. However, it supports intermodal.
  • Economic Sensitivity: J.B. Hunt is highly cyclical and vulnerable to broader economic slowdowns. This adds uncertainty.

data.title: Stryker's Growth Via Continuous Acquisitions Led To Declining ROIC
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past 12 years, Stryker Corporation (NYSE:SYK) has grown its revenue and net income at 8% CAGR. This growth came from a combination of organic growth and acquisitions. However, there was no corresponding uptrend in the returns. At the same time, the growth was funded by a high Reinvestment rate.

This is not a wonderful company in my opinion, as growth was mostly volume-driven. There was hardly any growth by price growth, suggesting that it may not have the best pricing power, raising questions about the strength of its moat. While it is financially ok, there is no margin of safety at the current market price in my view.

Business background

Stryker is one of the world's leading medical technology companies. In 2023, it had two reporting segments:

  • MedSurg and Neurotechnology. MedSurg products cover tools and technologies used in surgeries and patient care. Neurotechnology focuses on brain and nerve-related products such as instruments for brain surgery and implants for the skull.
  • Orthopedics and Spine. Orthopedic products include implants for joint replacements, like hips, knees, and shoulders, as well as tools for surgeries related to injuries and limbs. The Spine products include implants for the neck and lower back used in various spinal surgeries.

The MedSurg and Neurotechnology segment is the biggest revenue contributor in 2023. Refer to the right part of Chart 1. While the company has a global presence, the US accounted for the bulk of the revenue. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

Note to Chart 1: The segment profile for 2015 was compiled from the sales breakdown in the 2016 Form 10k.

Stryker operates in the Medtech sector. This is a mature sector with single-digit growth as illustrated by the following market research reports:

"The MedTech market size is projected to be worth USD 503 billion in 2023. The market is likely to surpass USD 777 billion by 2033 at a CAGR of 4.4% during the forecast period." Future Market Insights

"…the global MedTech…market size was valued at USD 574 billion in 2022 and is expected to expand at a CAGR of 6.0% during the forecast period, reaching USD 814 billion by 2028." Market Research Guru

Of course, the company does not operate in all the Medtech niches. Different niches have their specific growth rates that have to be seen in the context of the market size. The research reports seem to suggest that the bigger market niche had a lower growth rate, while the smaller niches had higher growth rates.

"The global orthopedic devices market size was estimated at USD 60 billion in 2023…is projected to grow at a CAGR of 4.3% from 2024 to 2030." Grand View Research

"The global neurotech devices market was valued at USD 13 billion and…growing at a CAGR of 13.5% from 2024 to 2031" Coherent Market Insights

"The global spinal devices market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.3% during the period 2022 to 2032…as of 2022, the market is valued at USD 13 billion." Future Market Insights

Given the above, you should not be surprised to find that Stryker's growth has been due to a combination of organic growth and acquisitions.

  • From 2012 to 2023, the company's revenue grew at 8.2% CAGR. I would attribute the growth to this combination.
  • In its Aug 2023 Investor Day presentation, the company had the following data on organic growth as shown in Chart 2. Based on the data presented, I estimated that the organic growth rate from 2012 to 2023 to be 8.1% CAGR.

If you compare the 2 growth rates, you would conclude that acquisitions only played a small role in the revenue growth over the past 12 years. I think this picture is a bit misleading.

This is because the company considered organic growth from the annual perspective as exemplified by the following quote from its 2023 Form 10k:

"…organic sales growth is calculated by translating current year and prior year results at the same foreign currency exchange rates excluding the impact of acquisitions and divestitures."

The company ignored the impact of its cumulative acquisitions carried out in the past. To give you a sense of the scale, from 2012 to 2023, the company incurred USD 5.4 billion in CAPEX compared to USD 19.5 billion in cash acquisitions.

Based on this and assuming a proportionate contribution to cumulative growth, I would think that acquisitions had a much bigger impact than organic growth.

This meant that to continue to grow at 8% CAGR, there must be sizeable acquisitions every now and then. This is not an unreasonable expectation as over the past 12 years, there were 5 years when the amount incurred on cash acquisition was more than USD 1 million for the year. The historical data showed that the big acquisition took place every 2 to 3 years.

Operating trends

From a revenue and PAT perspective, the performance of Stryker over the past 12 years has been commendable. Revenue grew at 8.2% CAGR while PAT grew at 8.4% CAGR. Refer to the left part of Chart 3. Note that the spike in profit in 2018 was due to a tax credit related to the transfer of intellectual properties between tax jurisdictions

Notwithstanding the profit uptrend, there was a contrasting picture of the returns. Refer to the right part of Chart 3.

  • ROIC and Cash flow return in invested capital declined from 2012 to 2023.
  • ROE in 2023 was at about the same level as that in 2012.

Nevertheless, from an overall perspective, the average ROIC over the past 12 years at 16% was higher compared to the current WACC of 8%. Similarly, the average ROE of 15% was higher than the current cost of equity of 8%. They indicate that shareholders' value was created.

Performance drivers

To get a sense of whether the improving revenue performance was due to price or volume changes, I tracked the price and volume index from 2012. The information to create this index was extracted from the respective Form 10k as illustrated by the following from the 2023 report.

"Excluding the 0.1% impact of acquisitions and divestitures, net sales in constant currency increased by 10.9% from increased unit volume and 0.6% due to higher prices."

You can see from the left part of Chart 5 that revenue growth was driven mostly by volume growth. There was hardly any price growth. I am not sure whether this is a good sign, given inflation over the past 12 years. It also suggests that the company may not have pricing power. If so, I would worry about its moat. I would have thought that if a company had a strong moat, it would have pricing power.

There are 2 other significant features. Refer to the right part of Chart 5.

  • Contribution margin was more or less stable despite the volume growth.
  • Fixed costs seemed to have a stable relationship with revenue. While the quantity of fixed costs had increased over the years, it was about 49% of revenue in 2023 compared to 48% in 2012.

Note that the fixed cost here included the R&D expenditure. I am not sure whether the R&D expenditure fully explains this link with revenue as over the past 12 years, the R&D expenditure averaged only 15% of the total cost.

It is hard to tell as an outsider. Other possible reasons for the fixed cost to vary with revenue can be gleaned from the 2023 Form 10k:

"…partially offset by higher selling, general and administrative expenses due to continued investments including sales growth incentives…."

"We expense sales commissions associated with obtaining a contract at the time of the sale or as incurred… These costs have been presented within selling, general, and administrative expenses…"

"We remeasure the fair value of our assets and liabilities each reporting period. We record the changes in fair value within selling, general, and administrative expenses."

The key takeaway is that in modeling the company, I will have to assume that the fixed cost behaves more like a variable cost.

Note to Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D, and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales - Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue - Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

Efficiencies

Looking at the left part of Chart 3, you can see that gross profitability (gross profits/total assets) declined over the past 12 years. In other words, there was no improvement in capital efficiency.

Along the same lines, the stable contribution margin suggests that there was no improvement in operating efficiency.

To see whether these efficiency findings are broad-based, I look at other operating and capital efficiency metrics.

  • Operating efficiencies. Refer to the left part of Chart 5. You can see that there was no improving trend for all the 4 metrics.
  • Capital efficiencies. Refer to the right part of Chart 5. I would conclude that there was no improving trend for all the 4 metrics.

I am not sure whether it is a positive sign or not. From a negative perspective, you could argue that as the company acquired companies, there were no operating or capital synergies.

Stryker benefited from new products and/or markets with the acquisition, but there was no benefit in the cost of doing business. Of course, you could argue that its cost did not get worse.

Reinvestments

Growth needs to be funded and one way to gauge this is to look at the Reinvestment rate. I defined this as Reinvestment / NOPAT.

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions - Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

From 2012 to 2023, Stryker had an average Reinvestment rate of 79%. This meant that there was only 21% of the NOPAT left for the shareholders. Note that if I exclude the acquisitions, the average Reinvestment rate reduces to 12%.

As such, I would expect its Reinvestment rate to have come down significantly in the long run. This would mean either smaller acquisitions or more cost-effective acquisitions. This may mean paying a lower price relative to the earnings potential, or looking for cost synergies as well.

Peer comparison

I compared its performance with 3 global Medtech companies as listed in Table 1.

Over the past 12 years, Stryker had the best revenue growth rates. But it was still the smallest company in the peer group when it came to the 2023 revenue. Nevertheless, the best growth rate suggests that it would have gained market share. Of course, you could argue that it bought the market share given its continuous acquisition history.

I looked at the trends of 4 metrics to get a sense of how well Stryker performed compared to its peers. Refer to Charts 6 and 7.

  • I would rate Stryker's return on capital and EBIT margin as average. The difference between Stryker and the peers in the EBIT margin had narrowed over the years. Looking at the trends, if Stryker does not pay attention to this metric, it may end up being the worst performer in the future.
  • I would consider its levered free cash flow margin as among the worst.
  • Its EPS seems to have improved relative to its peers.

Overall, I think that Stryker must focus on enhancing its cash flow and operational efficiency to avoid falling behind in a competitive landscape.

Financial position

I would assess Stryker's financial position as average as it had both positive and negative points. The main positive points are:

  • As of the end of Jun. 2024, it had USD 2.0 billion in cash and short-term investments. This was about 5% of its total assets.
  • From 2012 to 2023, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it generated about USD 27.5 billion in cash flow from operations, compared to a total PAT of USD 21.7 billion. This is a good cash conversion ratio.
  • It has a good capital allocation track record, as can be seen from Table 2. It was able to cover its CAPEX and acquisitions with the cash flow from operations. Excess was returned to shareholders.

There are 2 key negative points:

  • It had a 39% debt-capital ratio as of the end of Jun. 2024, although it has come down from its 2019 high of 52%. According to Damodaran Jan 2024 datasets, the healthcare products sector had a debt-capital ratio of 11%.
  • I have already mentioned the high average Reinvestment rate of 79%.

Given its strong cash flow generating track record, I would not worry too much about the debt-capital position. But the company has to address the high Reinvestment rate.

Valuation

The picture that emerged from my findings is a company operating in a sector with a single-digit market growth rate. Stryker had been able to deliver revenue growth at a higher rate due to its acquisitions. However, this resulted in a high Reinvestment rate that, I believe, should be lowered so that the company can return more earnings to shareholders.

While the company got bigger, its fixed cost has not come down. The track record suggests that its fixed cost is not so "fixed" but seems to be proportionate to revenue.

The company also does not have a track record of improving operating or capital efficiency.

As such, in valuing the company, I adopted the following assumptions.

  • The company will have another major acquisition next year that will enable it to deliver 14% revenue growth. This is the highest revenue growth over the past 12 years, excluding the growth immediately following the COVID-19 year.
  • Thereafter, revenue growth would decline proportionately to the terminal growth rate of 4% in Year 6.
  • It would be able to reduce its Reinvestment rate to about 30% in Year 6.
  • The operating and capital efficiency would follow the 2023 rate.
  • Its fixed cost would vary with revenue following the 2023 rate.

I valued Stryker using a multi-stage valuation model based on the above assumptions. I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 173 per share compared to its market price of USD 359 per share as of 16 Jun 2024. There is no margin of safety.

A comparison of the intrinsic value with other valuation metrics as shown in Table 3 supports the contention that there is no margin of safety.

  • You can see that with an Acquirer's Multiple of 31, it is not cheap
  • The Free Cash flow yield is well below my 5% cut-off.

Notes to Table 3.

a) EV / (average 3 years Op profit + Depreciation).

b) Past 10 years average Free cash flow/market price.

Valuation model

My valuation is based on the multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm model as shown in Table 4.

Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) = EBIT(1-t) X (1- Reinvestment rate).

Value of Firm = FCFF X (1 + g) / (WACC - g).

Value of Equity = Value of Firm + Cash - Total Debt - Minority Interests.

EBIT = Revenue X contribution margin - Fixed cost. The contribution margin and fixed cost are based on modeling the business as shown in the left part of Chart 4.

The operating efficiency is represented by the contribution margin.

The capital efficiencies are represented by the capital turnover (Revenue/TCE) and Reinvestment margin (Reinvestment/Revenue).

Notes to Table 4:Item (a) Straight line reduction.

Item (b) Pegged to the growth rate.

Item (c) Assumed no improvement.

Item (d) Assumed 49% of revenue.

Item (e) Revenue X Margin and after accounting for Fixed costs.

Item (f) Assumed to improve to achieve about 30% Reinvestment rate in the terminal year.

Item (g) = b X f

Item (h) FCFF for each year = e - g.

Item (j) Assumed constant D/E ratio. Refer to WACC Table 5.

Item (k) NPV for each year = (h X j).

Item (l) Terminal for the year discounted at terminal growth rate.

Item (m) 5 years NPV + terminal value.

Item (n) Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt as of Jun. 2024.

Item (o) Based on number of shares as of Jun. 2024.

Risks and limitations

There are 2 issues to consider when looking at my valuation.

  • Acquisition pattern.
  • Fixed cost.

The intrinsic value of Stryker is dependent on the acquisition pattern. In my model, I assumed that there would only be one major acquisition that would lead to the 14% jump in revenue in Year 1. Thereafter, the revenue growth rate would decline to the terminal rate in Year 6, indirectly suggesting that there is no further acquisition growth.

You may argue that this does not reflect what the company has done over the past 12 years. Stryker seems to have a continuous acquisition program, with several acquisitions greater than USD 2 billion every 2 to 3 years.

My valuation model does not cater to such a scenario. If you take this to the logical conclusion, it would mean that the company would continue to grow at its historical 8% forever. I am not sure whether this is realistic.

My point is that if you think that the company will have major acquisitions every 2 to 3 years, then you cannot rely on my valuation model. I am not sure whether the DCF valuation approach is appropriate in this context.

Given the big difference between my intrinsic value and the market price, I would deduce that the market is pricing Stryker on the basis that it would have continuous acquisitions moving forward. I would place this in the "too difficult to analyze" pile.

The other issue is the fixed cost. My analysis shows that historically the fixed cost is proportionate to revenue, averaging about 49% of revenue. This is very significant and if you assume that this can be brought down by 25%, you will have an intrinsic value equal to the market price.

But I am not sure whether this is possible given that there is no evidence of improving operating efficiency.

Conclusion

While Stryker is a profitable company with growing revenue and profits, I have several concerns:

  • Despite the profit growth, there was a declining ROIC and hardly any growth in ROE.
  • Growth was fueled by a high Reinvestment rate. The company cannot sustain this rate for a long period in my opinion.
  • There are no signs of improving operating or capital efficiencies.
  • Growth was mainly volume-driven. The lack of price-driven growth suggests that its moat may not be strong.

Nevertheless, the company is financially ok. A peer comparison seems to suggest that it had gained market share. While I would not consider it a wonderful company in the Buffett sense, it is not a cigar butt either.

The more worrisome point is that the market price seems far ahead of the business value. As such, I would not consider this an investment opportunity.

data.title: Airbus Deliveries Fall: Full Year Target At Risk?
data.description:
Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSY) published its overview of commercial airplane orders and deliveries for the month of September earlier this month. As I discuss in this report, the order inflow showed continued strength for Airbus' commercial airplane portfolio and strong demand from lessors and customers in Asia. However, on the delivery side, we're seeing challenges mount that could result in the European jet maker missing its delivery target for the year as aerospace supply chain challenges are becoming more prominently visible in the delivery volumes.

Airbus A321neo Leads The Order Inflow

In September, Airbus booked 235 orders valued at $14.5 billion. The mix consisted of 10 widebody airplanes and 225 single-aisle jets:

  • An undisclosed customer ordered 20 Airbus A320neos, 55 A321neos, five Airbus A350-900s and five Airbus A350-1000s.
  • CDB Leasing ordered 15 Airbus A320neos and 65 Airbus A321neos.
  • Cebu Pacific ordered 70 Airbus A321neos.

During the month, the following order book changes and mutations took place:

  • Frontier Group Holdings, Inc. (ULCC) converted orders for 22 A320neos to 22 Airbus A321neos while slots for an additional 14 A320neos were transferred to BOC Aviation and one delivery slot was transferred to ICBC Leasing.
  • Azul Finance was identified as the customer for one Airbus A321neo, which was subsequently converted to an order for the A320neo.
  • Chengdu Airlines converted an order for one A321neo to one A320neo.
  • Juneyao Air was identified as the customer for one A321neo.
  • Sichuan Airlines converted an order for two A319neos to two A320neos.
  • Tibet Airlines was identified as the customer for one Airbus A319neo.
  • An undisclosed customer converted orders for eight Airbus A319neos to eight A321neos.

Airbus announced orders from "only" three customers, but they were big orders. The order for 85 airplanes from an unidentified customer can likely be linked to Air India firming options while the orders from CDB and Cebu show healthy and continued demand for the Airbus A320neo family and more specifically the A321neo. Also interesting to note is that cancellations remain low. I believe that's partially driven by the problems that Boeing is facing combined with the delivery delays that Airbus has, which makes delivery slots even more valuable. Instead of deliveries being cancelled, it is likely that Airbus is engaging with customers to reschedule deliveries which partially drives the conversion activity to other models at attractive terms.

In the same month last year, Airbus booked 23 orders and no cancellations, bringing net orders to 23 airplanes valued at $2.3 billion, showing that year-on-year the order activity increased significantly. Each of the orders placed in September was already higher than the combined order inflow in September 2023. Year-to-date, Airbus booked 667 orders and 19 cancellations, bringing its net orders to 648 airplanes valued at $55 billion. A year ago, the European jet maker booked 1,280 gross orders and 39 cancellations, bringing the net order tally to 1,241 units valued at $84.4 billion. Year-to-date we see a strong decline in order inflow driven by large non-recurring orders placed last year.

Airbus Airplane Deliveries Continue To Decrease Year-on-Year

Airbus delivered a total of 50 airplanes in September valued at $3.1 billion:

  • Five Airbus A220 airplanes were delivered.
  • A total of 41 Airbus A320neo airplanes were delivered, including one Airbus A319neo, 12 Airbus A320neo airplanes and 28 Airbus A321neo airplanes.
  • Airbus delivered one Airbus A330-900neo.
  • There were three Airbus A350 airplane deliveries, two for the -900 model and one for the -1000 model.

During the same month last year, Airbus delivered 55 airplanes valued at $3.6 billion. Year-over-year, we saw deliveries decrease by five units driven by lower deliveries on all its programs except for the A220 program.

Year-to-date, Airbus has delivered 497 airplanes valued at $31.7 billion, compared to 488 deliveries valued at $31.2 billion a year ago. So, deliveries are higher year-on-year but not as much as one would have expected, and for the full year Airbus now expects deliveries to climb by 5% instead of 9%. The year-to-date numbers show a 1.8% increase in deliveries. When Airbus announced a reduction to the delivery target earlier this year, it caught me off guard as there still was quite some time left in the year. However, we're now seeing that year-on-year the deliveries are lower and Airbus had anticipated that challenging path into the final months of the year.

The book-to-bill ratio was 4.7x in terms of units and value. Year-to-date, the book-to-bill ratio in terms of units also were 1.3x and 1.8x in terms of value reflecting strong bookings partially amplified by somewhat underwhelming delivery volumes.

Conclusion: Airbus Deliveries Show Aerospace Supply Chain Challenges

The order activity shows continued demand for Airbus airplanes with September's order inflow showing the appeal of Airbus airplanes in Asia and among lessors. However, at this point, we're not so much focused on orders. Airbus has a more than comfortable backlog and the problem is actually on the delivery side. We're seeing year-on-year declines in deliveries reflecting continued challenges in the supply chain. The supply chain challenges are stickier than initially thought and in fact, you could say that the supply chain challenges resurfaced instead of easing. On average, Airbus needs to deliver 91 airplanes per month, and while the company has been able to deliver more than 100 airplanes in a single month, it has only been able to do so in the final month of the year with significantly lower deliveries in the months prior. Last year, the fourth quarter accounted for a third of the full-year deliveries. Using that distribution in the current year, we would get to 753 deliveries. It shows that Airbus needs to ramp up deliveries significantly in order to not miss its delivery target of 770 airplanes. While I definitely do not think that Airbus management is doing a bad job, it appears that the company is putting delivery targets that are not reflective of the aerospace supply chain health.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arvinas: Major Catalyst Ahead, But Its History Gives Me Pause
data.description:
I have been covering Arvinas, Inc. (NASDAQ:ARVN) for a long time, and I see that while I began with positive coverage back in 2021, my view has turned negative to neutral over the years. I also note that my negative articles have fared better than the positive ones in terms of the price movement since publication. That fact, validated over the last 4 years, tells me my negative opinion about ARVN is probably more correct. However, it has been a while since I last covered it, so let me see if there’s any change here.

Background

Arvinas was incubated at Yale University. Its technology is proteolysis targeting chimeras, or PROTAC protein degraders. The body has a natural disposal system for waste proteins. This system recognizes harmful misfolded and mutated proteins through E3 ligases. These disposal-ready proteins are then tagged with ubiquitin. Proteasomes recognize proteins tagged with ubiquitin as ready for disposal. They break down and degrade these tagged proteins into smaller peptides and dispose of them.

Yale’s Craig Crews and team discovered a way to hijack this system for therapeutic use. Here they use PROTACs to tag the pathologic proteins. PROTAC brings the harmful proteins near the ubiquitin-proteasome system, and makes them ready for disposal.

The lead candidate a few years ago was ARV-110 targeting mCRPC or metastatic chemotherapy resistant prostate cancer, and a second contender used to be ARV-471 in ER+/HER2- breast cancer. 110 has been removed from the pipeline after poor data that I covered earlier, and 471, now also known as Vepdegestrant, has become the lead molecule. It is partnered with Pfizer since 2021, and is in phase 3 trials.

ARV-471 data

ARV-471 is being developed by Arvinas in collaboration with Pfizer and is currently being evaluated as a monotherapy in the second-line setting in an ongoing phase 3 trial called VERITAC-2. It is also being developed in the first-line setting in combination with palbociclib in the ongoing Phase 3 VERITAC-3 trial.

I discussed 471’s early data in my December 2022 article. That was from the phase 1 expansion cohort of a phase 1/2 study called VERITAC, evaluating ARV-471 monotherapy and in combination with palbociclib. The data didn’t impress; clinical benefit rate of 38% was seen, with poor CR/PR figures. Out of 71 patients, only two had a partial response, none had a complete response, and the best we had was stable disease.

Another problem, at least until then, was that the CBR in ESR1 wild-type patients was much lower than in ESR1 mutant patients. Approved oral selective estrogen receptor degraders, or SERDs, work well in ESR1 mutant patients, not so well in ESR1 wild-type patients. So this did not give 471 good positioning in the ESR1 wild-type population.

Moreover, 471 did not fare too well when compared to SERDs in terms of median PFS, which was 3.5 months for 471 and have shown results as high as 6.5 months for some SERDs. Furthermore, as I noted at that time, a key SERD molecule, AstraZeneca’s AZN) faslodax, just went generic, putting pressure in the space.

Lastly, the AE profile wasn’t too good either, with 5 patients seeing grade 3/4 AEs, with 2 discontinuations. This is where we left 471 at that time, and moved ahead with 110. However, 110 was let go at some point, and 471 produced more data.

In December, the company released interim phase 1 data from the same combo drugs in 46 heavily pre-treated locally advanced or metastatic ER+/HER2- breast cancer patients with a median of four lines of therapy. Data showed that the combo did well in both wild-type and mutant settings, in both CBR and ORR, as well as in mPFS. CBR rate was 63% in general, and for the RP3D (dosage for phase 3) of 200mg, CBR was 67%. In ESR1 mutant patients, CBR was 72/5, and at 200mg RP3D it was 79%. In wild-type ESR1 patients, CBR was 53%, however, for 200mg, this was only 43%. Thus, in the wild type patients, 200mg didn’t do as well as the other doses, while none of the doses did as well as with the mutant populations.

Coming to ORR, it was 42% broadly and 53% at RP3D. For mutant ESR1, it was 47% broadly and 60% for RP3D. For wild-type ESR1, ORR was 42% broadly and 40% for RP3D, demonstrating the relatively poor show in wild-type ESR1.

Another metric tested was mPFS, which was 11.1 months and remained almost the same across mutation status.

Neutropenia was the most problematic AE, and grade 4 neutropenia was seen in 38% patients at the RP3D of vepdegestrant (200 mg) plus palbociclib 125 mg. Grade 3/4 neutropenia occurred in 89% of all patients. Much of this AE can be attributed to palbociclib.

Several analysts, including Jefferies, Wells Fargo and others, rated this data very positively. Goldman’s Paul Choi, while expecting positive phase 3 data slated for late-2024, said that such data “should lead to vepdegestrant emerging as the next standard of care in 2L+ ER+/HER2- breast cancer.”

Financials

ARVN has a market cap of $1.78bn and a cash balance of $1.23bn as of June. Including in this figure is the $150mn they received from Novartis (NVS) in May as licensing upfront fees for ARV-766. Research and development expenses were $93.7 million for the quarter ended June 30, 2024, while general and administrative expenses were $31.3 million. At that cash burn rate, they have a cash runway of 8–9 quarters.

Risks

I tend to agree with Citi, which sees therapeutic potential in ARV-471, but still considers Arvinas, Inc. stock’s price tag quite high. Of course, countering that, it can be said that their very high cash balance evens it out. It gives them an enterprise value of just over half a billion dollars, which, given Citi’s own estimate of vepdeg peak sales, could be considered low.

Bottom line

ARVN has not done well if we consider the entire period of time I have been covering it; however, in the last one-year term, it has done very well. This has been possible due mainly to the latest data; the earlier poor show was because of the poor earlier data.

I would like to put some money in ARVN based on the phase 3 data drop that should occur soon (trial is fully enrolled). However, I will still like to give this some more thought before I do so. My reluctance is mainly because that one set of positive data contrasts starkly with a lot of poor data we have seen from this company’s pipeline.

data.title: QQQ: Time To Take Some Profit
data.description:
QQQ ETF hovers near all-time high

I last covered the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) back in August. The article was titled “QQQ: What If A Soft Landing Happens?” As you can already guess from the title, the article focused on the impacts of a soft landing scenario on QQQ’s return potential. More specifically, I argued for a buy rating based on the following factors:

QQQ's risk premium relative to risk-free rates has subsided noticeably in the past month. Recent macroeconomic data suggest better odds for a soft-landing scenario. A soft landing would benefit other funds besides QQQ, of course. However, my experience is that lower interest rates and steady GDP growth tend to benefit tech-oriented stocks.

Indeed, QQQ has recovered from its recent corrections, as seen in the next chart. The ETF currently hovers near an all-time high price and has delivered a terrific total return of over 14% YTD.

Since that writing, the economic conditions have changed substantially and the odds for a soft landing have diminished drastically in my view, judging by the latest job data, CPI data, and also market expectations for continued rate cuts in the near future. The remainder of this article will elaborate on these changes. You will see why these changes have led me to lower my rating from the previous Buy to Hold. My recommendation is that QQQ investors should take advantage of the price rebound and take some profit.

QQQ ETF: Basic facts

As one of the most popular funds, QQQ needs little introduction. So here I will limit myself to only a few features that are directly relevant to the rest of my analysis. Readers new to the fund should look at its fund description for more details.

Given its tech and growth orientation, QQQ has significant exposure to the information technology sector, as you can see from the chart below. This sector alone accounts for 50.44% of its total holdings and features exposure to the likes of Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Nvidia (NVDA), etc. However, note that the fund also has sizable exposures to non-tech sectors such as consumer staples (at 6.08% weight) and healthcare (at 5.98% weight).

The next chart outlines the key characteristics of the QQQ ETF. QQQ holds a total of 101 underlying securities, which are the 101 largest non-financial companies in the NASDAQ-100 Index. The ETF charges a reasonable total expense ratio of 0.20% (I will revisit this later). Here I want to bring your attention to the valuation metrics. As seen, its price-to-book (P/B) ratio stands at 16.10 and its price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is at 37.66. These multiples entail considerable valuation risks, in my view, given the current interest rate outlook, as elaborated on next.

QQQ ETF: Valuation risks under new rate outlook

Risk-free rates serve as the gravity of all asset valuation, and QQQ is no exception. The outlook for interest rates has changed drastically since my last writing as new job and CPI data were released. Specifically, the following CME Group FedWatch Tool shows that the current market expectation for continued aggressive rate cuts has subsided substantially. Even just a month ago, the market expected a large probability of 41.3% for the Fed rates to be lowered to the 400 to 425 basis points range by the end of 2024. In contrast, the market sees no possibility for this scenario at all. Such a drastic change has caused a material shift in QQQ’s risk premium.

As a reflection of the heightened risk premium, the chart below shows the yield spread between QQQ and 10-year Treasury bond rates (which I consider to be the benchmark risk-free rates). As seen, the yield spread is currently at negative 3.465%, noticeably lower than the negative ~3.2% at the time of my previous writing.

More importantly, given the rate cut outlook at that time, the yield spread could widen to about -2.8% by the end of 2024, on par with the historical average in the long term as seen. However, given the new rate cut outlook, the yield spread is very likely to persist at the currently very negative level, translating into much a higher risk premium in the new year or so.

Other risks and final thoughts

As another reflection of the risk premium, QQQ’s P/E currently hovers around 38x, as aforementioned in the fund introduction section. Such a multiple is quite elevated either in absolute terms or relative terms. To better contextualize things, the following chart (taken from Full Ratio) shows the historical P/E of the NASDAQ index in the past 10 years. As seen, the highest P/E observed was about 30x in the past (if you consider the 106x observed in 2016 to be an outlier). The current P/E of QQQ is about 27% above the peak P/E observed for the index in the past 10 years.

On the positive side, QQQ features some of the most innovative and profitable stocks in its portfolio. If you recall from an earlier chart, the return on equity - ROE - for the fund is about 43%. To provide a reference point, the ROE for the general economy as represented by the S&P 500 index is “only” about 20%, less than half of the companies underlying QQQ. Such a high ROE can certainly justify some degree of valuation premium.

As a final note, buy-and-hold investors could consider QQQ’s sister fund, the Invesco Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQM). Both funds track the NASDAQ-100 Total Return Index. The main difference between the two funds is threefold, in my view. The first one is that QQQM has a lower expense ratio than QQQ (0.15% vs. 0.20%) as seen from the chart below. The second difference is that QQQM has a much lower AUM and trading volume than QQQ as seen. The combination of lower fees and higher tradability makes QQQM an ideal alternative for buy-and-hold investors. The final difference is that QQQM is priced at less than half of the price of QQQ ($202 per share vs. $490 per share as of this writing), making it easier for smaller accounts to manage their holdings.

To sum up, the goal of this article is to reexamine the return/risk profile of QQQ given the developments since my last writing. The key development, in my view, is the drastically different rate cut outlook. This changed outlook, when combined with QQQ’s valuation premium due to the recent price rebounds, has elevated QQQ’s risk premium sufficiently for a change of rating from my earlier Buy to Hold.

data.title: Celsius: Growth Story Isn't Dead
data.description:
Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) has gone from hero to laggard in just months. The energy drink company still appears to have a bright future, despite the 70% dip since the all-time highs in just May. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bullish on the stock due to several catalysts to grow and the eventual tailwind of confusing sales data.

Puzzling Sales Data

The whole energy drink sector has been under pressure the last few months. Data continues to show sales have slumped in the sector after a period of solid growth.

Celsius has been further hit by confusing sales data from major distribution partner PepsiCo. (PEP). The soft drink giant became the domestic distribution partner of Celsius back at the end of 2022 leading to massive sales growth.

Unfortunately, the sales growth was somewhat misleading due to Pepsi building up inventory. Now, as the company is more familiar with the ordering pattern of healthy energy drink customers, Pepsi has reduced the inventory levels on hand to boost cash flows.

The end result is that Celsius reported higher sales growth initially and now the company is reporting lower sales than actual sales through data supports. Celsius reported Q2'24 sales of $402 million, up 23% YoY.

The issue here is that sales have generally stagnated since Q3'23 when Celsius reached sales of $385 million. The big hiccup is where Pepsi sales impact the actual sales picture.

Back at the Piper Sandler conference, management discussed Pepsi reducing inventory levels by another $100 to $120 million in Q3 alone versus the prior-year period. Along with the Q2 earnings call about a month prior, Celsius discussed the Pepsi impact as follows:

So I mentioned in the prepared remarks and back in June that there was kind of a potential $20 million to $30 million headwind because of the DOH changes. So that ended up on the back half of – so the lower end of that range, so more in the 20 to 25 range as opposed to the higher end of the range. So that's where we ended the quarter when it relates to that piece.

Celsius should provide more detail on the Pepsi inventory issue, but so far, the company only provides these off the cuff remarks lacking precise details. The best view is that Celsius saw a $20+ million hit to Q2 sales, where the reported sales of $402 million would actually be $422 million. The whole picture would appear to add up to a roughly $150 million cumulative inventory reduction by Pepsi so far in 2024, though the complete picture isn't clear.

The energy drink company continues to report strong growth, but the data definitely further questions the sell through rate going forward. Nielsen data showed a 13% increase in volume with a nearly 6% price cut for the last 4 weeks.

Of course, this data excludes some of the growth catalysts like international and the club channel, where Amazon sales growth remains strong. Celsius will continue reporting overall solid sales growth, if not for the uncertain Pepsi inventory drag.

Hidden Catalyst

The international growth catalyst hasn't even started to hit the quarterly numbers yet. Celsius entered Canada in January to provide a boost, but sales outside of North America still remains rather low.

The energy drink company is working on entering Australia, New Zealand and France after launching in the U.K. and Ireland this year, initially just in fitness stores and gyms. The company only reported Q2 international sales of just shy of $20 million, less than 5% of total sales.

In reality, international sales growth only grew slightly below $5 million YoY due to some historical sales amounts. Monster Beverage (MNST) does nearly $800 million in quarterly international sales.

The international market is only 41% of the Monster Beverage business, and it's still far larger than the whole business of Celsius. The energy drink company focused on health and wellness still has a long growth trajectory, though competition like Alani Nu is eating into some of the growth potential, with volumes up 78% in the last period.

What makes the stock far more appealing now is that the market is pricing in limited growth for the energy drink company. Celsius still shows signs of under the surface growth.

The consensus analyst estimates have Q3 revenues plunging to $272 million, followed by rebounds going forward. The flip side of the weak numbers due to Pepsi is that Celsius will report higher growth next year, and the analysts' estimates appear very conservative.

As an example, the normalized Q2 revenues were at least $422 million. Assuming Pepsi purchases based on end user demand in 2025, Celsius revenue estimates of only $436 million is just $14 million sales growth YoY.

Even the Nielsen data still has Celsius sales growing, plus the company has all of the international growth opportunities ahead. Just normalized 5% sales growth would place Q3'25 revenue estimates at closer to $443 million, already $9 million above analyst estimates.

The stock now trades at just 5x forward sales. Monster Beverage still trades at over 6x forward sales, while normally trading closer to 7x to 8x when growing at the rate of Celsius.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Celsius has likely bottomed out for the Pepsi inventory issues. The stock should start building a base here around $30 and any signs of the inventory issue being over and the energy drink company back to reporting actual growth will send Celsius higher.

data.title: RTX Corporation: I Maintain A Buy Rating Despite Apparent Imbalance In Defense
data.description:
The financial year for the Department of Defense ended on the 30th of September. That provides a good moment to see how much contract value has been secured by primary defense contractors. The reason why I believe it's important to assess contracting activity for individual companies is because generally the view is that expanding defense budgets provide investors and analysts with a generic bull case for defense contractors. However, not all companies are seeing significant growth. In fact, out of all the defense contractors for which I have analyzed the contracting activity in the past DoD fiscal year, we saw that troubled aerospace and defense giant The Boeing Company (BA) was the only company that saw a significant increase in contracting activity while General Dynamics Corporation (GD) and Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) saw contracting activity decline.

In this report, I will be focusing on the contract flow for RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX). The report will not be focused on a price target or rating for the stock. If you're interested in a price target assessment, I refer to a previous report discussing the earnings and valuation of RTX Corp.

RTX Corporation Contract Value Declines But Not At An Alarming Rate

Using the evoX Defense Contracts Monitor, The Aerospace Forum collects defense contract data for more than 100 companies and RTX Corporation is one of the companies that we cover. The database for which I have developed a visualizing software contains more than 11,000 contracts, which we leverage to cover aerospace and defense companies on a more detailed level. RTX Corporation secured $20.2 billion worth of contracts during the DoD fiscal year, which was a reduction of 26.6%. However, the number of contracts declined only 11.6%, showing that the decline was driven by a combination of lower volumes and lower value per contract. Obligated funds declined by 32.7% to $9.8 billion.

In total, there has been a $7.3 billion reduction in contract value. Interestingly, using our data analytics tools we can actually pinpoint the specific programs that caused the reduction, and it's not as bad as it seems. The F-35 program saw a $2.6 billion decline in contracting activity, and given the challenges Lockheed Martin faced on the F-35 program this year getting the TR-3 or Technology Refresh 3 ready, a lower contract value does not really come as a surprise. Furthermore, there is always some lumpiness in the contracting activity for the program, and it takes time for demand to translate to firm contracts. So, in many cases, the increased interest in the F-35 is not yet reflected in the order activity.

Furthermore, last year there was a $5.5 billion framework contract that RTX won for services on the F117 engine which is used on the C-17 Globemaster III, and the Patriot system saw a decline in contracting activity of $1 billion. This was partially offset by higher radar sales and missile defense contracts of which the LTAMDS experienced the most notable contract growth.

There's also a different way to look at the changes and that's by excluding the IDIQs which are indefinite-delivery/indefinite-quantity contracts. These contract vehicles provide a framework against which task and delivery orders can be placed. If we exclude these contract vehicles, the decline in the value of the contracts is around 21%.

RTX Corporation Has A Diversified Product Portfolio

One thing that I definitely do like about RTX Corporation is the fact that its order inflow is nicely diversified. Missiles and missile defense account for the majority of the company's contract flow and that's a part of the defense business where we increased demand due to the development of new weapon system capabilities such as hypersonics. And missile defense and radars are also higher in demand as the increased threat levels are triggering US and US allies to increase defense capabilities. So, the missile, missile defense and radar and communications & intelligence parts of the defense space show the defensive stance as well as the demand for the development of deterrents. Furthermore, via engine platforms, the company has significant exposure to aircraft including fighter jets such as the F-35 for which demand is increasing as more and more countries are increasing budgets. Previously, the F-35 was deemed too expensive for many countries. But with increased defense budgets, the F-35 now becomes an option for many countries and that provides RTX with contracts to provide the engines and a long-term sustained tail.

By the end of the second quarter, RTX had a backlog of $206 billion, of which $77 billion is attributable to the defense business. For RTX, it's rather difficult to assess whether the current backlog is a comfortable one and the reason for that is that its reporting segments are not pure commercial sales or pure defense sales. However, when we look at the annualized sales for the Raytheon segment, which is the best reflection of performance in the defense business, we see that annualized sales should be around $26.35 billion. I consider a backlog of 2x annual sales to be comfortable, and RTX Corporation actually gets to 2.9x and that's when we don't account for military sales for Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney. Adding the military sales of the other segments, we get to $14.2 billion in annualized sales. Adding this to the $26.35 billion for Raytheon gives us $40.6 billion in annualized sales and brings the backlog-to-sales to 1.9x. So, the ratio is slightly below the 2x targeted, but I don't think it is alarming as the company also has IDIQs which could materialize into firm contracts. However, I do note that measured against the contract flow observed in the past DoD fiscal year the book-to-bill ratio only reaches 0.5x. So, RTX actually needs a significant step-up in contract activity to maintain a comfortable cushion.

Conclusion: Increased Demand For Defense Equipment Not Clearly Visible For RTX

What holds for RTX is that it has a diversified defense portfolio, but its $77 billion backlog falls short of the comfortable cushion of 2x and its order inflow for defense contracts has been significantly lower. The Raytheon segment, which is fully dedicated to defense, has a 12-month rolling book-to-bill ratio of 1.13x, signaling healthy demand. But I believe that the company requires a significantly higher order inflow to maintain a comfortable cushion and execute growth in the defense business. So, with that in mind, I'm turning a bit more cautious on RTX regarding growth in the defense business, but I maintain bullish on the overall combination of defense and commercial sales.

data.title: Wave Life Sciences: Positive WVE-006 Data For AATD Leads To 2025 Catalyst
data.description:
Wave Life Sciences (NASDAQ:WVE) announced positive results from its phase 1b/2a RestorAAtion-2 study using WVE-006 for the treatment of patients with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency [AATD]. It was shown that a single subcutaneous administration of 200 mg of this therapy was able to result in the presence of wild-type alpha-1 antitrypsin [M-AAT] protein. This ended up resulting in the creation of more than 60% of total AAT protein. This is very good news, but because this catalyst has passed doesn't mean that there isn't another catalyst to look forward to for this program. That is, the company expects to release additional data from this very same mid-stage study in 2025. Besides this, there is another candidate who recently had news released as well. This would be with respect to a clinical candidate by the name of WVE-003 for the treatment of patients with Huntington's Disease.

The company has been able to report a statistically significant reduction of cerebral spinal fluid [CSF] mHTT protein for them. However, despite this achievement, Takeda Pharmaceutical (TAK) chose not to exercise its option for this program anyways. On the flip side, Wave Life Sciences now has full rights to this program. Not only that, but it is still expected to meet with the FDA before the end of 2024 to discuss the possibility of an Accelerated Approval pathway for it as well. With additional results from the phase 1b/2a RestorAAtion-2 study using WVE-006 for AATD patients, plus the possibility of having an Accelerated Approval pathway of WVE-003 for the treatment of patients with HD, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

WVE-006 Has Another Expected Milestone To Unlock Further Value

As I noted above, Wave Life Sciences is developing the use of WVE-006 for the treatment of patients with alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency in the ongoing phase 1b/2a RestorAAtion-2 study. Before going over this mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to come from this program, it is first important to understand what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be. Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency [AATD] is an inherited disorder that may cause lung or liver disease. What happens is that these patients don't have enough AAT protein to protect both of these organs. Why is that? It is because such patients inherit two copies of the SERPINA1 gene. This gene causes the formation of abnormal AAT proteins and thus, functioning AAT protein levels are not ideal. The global Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency market is expected to reach a $10 billion market value by 2033. This is a large market opportunity and the company is well on its way to potentially capture it. Especially, with the positive data it has released from this study.

In order to see if WVE-006 is capable of being able to treat patients with AATD, it is in the process of running the phase 1b/2a RestorAAtion-2 study. This trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 24 patients who are to be dosed with WVE-006. The thing to note is that the data just released today only deals with a single subcutaneous dose of this GalNAc-conjugated drug. Having said that, even though this was just a single dose, two patients were able to achieve more than 60% of total AAT protein levels. Again, this is very good news, because it goes back to the notion that these patients lack AAT. Not only that, but there was the presence of M-ATT protein confirmed. Why is this crucial? It is because this shows that there was successful editing of mutant Z-AAT mRNA. In turn, the ability to generate 50% M-AAT in this heterozygous "MZ" genotype patients, means lower risk of AATD liver and lung disease. The thing is that there might be an opportunity for the data to eventually be improved upon. That's because the expectation is that the company expects to release multi-dose WVE-006 data, targeting these patients with AATD, in 2025. If the single dose data was able to achieve this, then it is quite possible that the multi-dose data may end up doing better.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Wave Life Sciences had cash and cash equivalents of $154 million as of June 30th of 2024. The projection was that it would have enough cash to fund its operations into Q4 of 2025. This is about a little over 1-year's worth of cash runway, having said that the company decided to raise additional funds for its operations. Having said that, it was able to complete an upsized public offering to raise approximately $230 million before deducting expenses.

An important item to mention is that this doesn't include any potential milestone and other payments under the collaboration agreement it has with GlaxoSmithKline (GSK). Both of these companies are advancing WVE-006 for the treatment of patients with AATD together in a partnership. Thus, once this phase 1b/2a RestorAAtion-2 study concludes, then from there, GlaxoSmithKline will be responsible for development and commercialization of this drug for the treatment of this patient population. If this program goes well, then Wave Life Sciences is eligible to receive up to $525 million in milestone payments along with tiered royalties on net sales.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Wave Life Sciences. The first risk to consider deals with the ongoing phase 1b/2a RestorAATion-2 study, which is using WVE-006 for the treatment of patients with AATD. Even though the initial subcutaneous single-dose data turned out to be positive, there is no guarantee that the multi-dose results to be released in 2025 will turn out to be positive as well. Nor, that the efficacy of the drug will be improved upon with further dosing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of WVE-003 for the treatment of patients with HD. That's because the goal is to bring the data established thus far to regulatory authorities to see if it can obtain an Accelerated Approval pathway. The risk here is that there is no assurance that one or any regulatory agency will allow for an Accelerated Approval pathway based on the clinical evidence obtained thus far.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the advancement of WVE-007 for the treatment of patients with obesity. The goal is to develop this drug either alone as a monotherapy or in combination with a GLP-1 agonist to treat them. This oligonucleotide is a small interfering RNA [GalNAc-siRNA] to silence the Inhibin βE gene. If this is silenced, then it would induce fat-burning along with muscle mass preservation. The risk is that there is no assurance that the drug will work either alone or in combination with a GLP-1 agonist to treat the obesity patients. Plus, a phase 1 study using WVE-007 to treat these patients is expected to begin in 2025. Upon completion of this trial, there is no guarantee that the results will turn out to be positive.

Conclusion

Wave Life Science has done well to prove that its RNA editing platform is capable of treating a variety of disorders. It has shown to do well in treating these patients with Huntington's Disease [HD] by showing the ability of WVE-003 to reduce CSF mHTT protein by 46%. Even though Takeda didn't exercise its option to take over this program, there is still a milestone in play for investors to consider. In that, the company is expected to meet with multiple regulatory agencies to see if it can obtain an Accelerated Approval pathway for this program before the end of 2024. It has also shown its technology to help patients with AATD as well. Not only were positive results just released today using WVE-006 to treat these patients, but there is another data set expected from it in 2025. I believe that this biotech has established proof of mechanism of action [MOA] in several disorders. Thus, I believe it has set itself up for long-term success.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nature's Sunshine Products: Cheap, But Not Entirely Without A Reason To Be
data.description:
Self-care is becoming increasingly important to everyday Americans. In fact, globally, it is more important now than ever. In the US alone, in 2022, about 50% of consumers reported wellness as a top priority in their day-to-day lives. That's up from 42% just two years earlier. This is especially the case when it comes to younger generations. Millennials, for instance, are very wellness oriented. 52% of them stated that they have a high prioritization that they are assigning to health and wellness. That compares to 47% for all consumers. Better nutrition comes in at 48% compared to 40% for consumers. And better appearance is at 37% compared to 30% for consumers. In fact, in 2022, the total US wellness products market was worth an estimated $450 billion. At each year, it's expected to grow by about 5%.

In the Asia Pacific part of the world, the overall industry is even larger. While the total North American wellness market is worth about $1.3 trillion, in the Asia Pacific region, it's a massive $1.5 trillion. In fact, in Asia, wellness is such a big thing that it has even been the epicenter of what is now known as the wellness tourism industry. One company that is focused very much on Wellness, not only throughout North America, but also in Asia and elsewhere, is Nature's Sunshine Products (NASDAQ:NATR). With a market capitalization of only $243.8 million, the business is quite small. However, it has a robust balance sheet and shares are trading at remarkably low multiples. The one downside to this is that financial results are volatile and have exhibited no signs of real growth over the past few years. But even with these weak spots, the stock looks cheap enough to maintain as a soft ‘buy’.

A wellness check on this name

Whenever I write an article about a company for the first time, I like to dig in and really discuss what the company is and what it does. For those not familiar with Nature's Sunshine Products, management describes the firm as a natural health and wellness business. It's focused largely on the production of nutritional and personal care products. These are sold directly to customers. But they are also sold to a sales force of independent consultants, who then resell those products to customers.

While the wellness space is truly vast in scope, I think it would be appropriate to use that same description for Nature's Sunshine Products and what it offers. Based on what management has said, the company sells over 800 products that touch on the immune system, cardiovascular health, digestive health, general personal care, weight management, and more. Using herbs and other things, the company creates and then packages these products that it then sells to its customers. For all intents and purposes, these products can be classified as dietary supplements.

The supplement industry represents only a very small piece of the overall global wellness market. However, it is a space that has been growing at a nice rate. From 2019 through 2023, sales expanded from $142.5 million to $182.7 million. That's an annualized growth rate of 6.4%. This year, the market should grow further to $192.4 million. And by 2026, it's expected to hit $214 million. Usually small companies like this that are based in the US get most of their sales from the US. Or if not, at least they get most of their sales from North America. However, Nature's Sunshine Products is an exception to this. In 2023, 45.2% of the company's sales came from Asia. North America had the second-largest contribution at 31.4%. Europe came in third at 18.2%. And the rest of the world, comprising mostly of the Latin America region, accounted for the remaining 5.2% of sales.

In the chart above, you can see growth that the company has achieved from a revenue perspective by region. Asia has been the major source of expansion for the company during this time. In 2021, the company generated $176.9 million in revenue from there. By 2023, that number had grown to $201.3 million. Unfortunately, other parts of the world have not been as kind for Nature's Sunshine Products as Asia has. But starting in 2023, the firm did begin to see improvements in both North America and Europe. Unfortunately, for the current fiscal year, Asia has started to be a bit of a problem. This was mostly because of weakness in China caused by what management described as reduced activity coming as a result of challenging macroeconomic factors.

What matters most to me is that the business generates revenue from all of the most important parts of the world for this industry. In 2023, 36% of all global supplement sales came from North America. But Asia and Oceania were close behind at 34%. Europe and the Middle East are still large markets, accounting for a combined 19% of the space. And Latin America is responsible for about 10% of sales.

When it comes to Nature's Sunshine Products on its own, overall financial performance has been mixed in recent years. As I mentioned earlier, weakness in key markets, particularly in North America and Europe, caused revenue to drop from $444.1 million in 2021 to $421.9 million in 2022.
