A recovery in both North America and Europe, combined with continued strong growth in Asia that management attributed to a continued dedication to growing its sales force in markets like Taiwan and Japan, as well as to the firm furthering its digitally focused, livestream business model in China, was responsible for an expansion in sales to $445.3 million last year.

On the bottom line, as the chart above illustrates, financial performance has been all over the map. Net profits and cash flows have shown no real trend year over year. In fact, in both 2022 and 2023, adjusted operating cash flow remained flat at $28 million compared to the $53.5 million reported for 2021, while EBITDA for the company rebounded somewhat from 2022. To be honest with you, the profit and cash flow picture for the business does concern me more than anything else. Some of this, like a good chunk of the weakness experienced in 2022, can be attributed to really high-income taxes. That year, the effective tax rate for the business was an astounding 96.4%. But other factors have also proven problematic as well, namely an increase in selling, general, and administrative costs caused by higher compensation, marketing, and certain growth oriented variable costs.

If we were seeing rapid growth, I would actually be fine with some of this short-term pain. However, that is not the case. For the first half of this year, revenue was $221.5 million. That's down 1.6% compared to the $225.2 million reported one year earlier. This was mostly because of the aforementioned weakness in its Asia operations that management chalked up to China. Admittedly, the company did see its net profits improve from $3.3 million to $3.7 million. But other profitability metrics have been mixed. Operating cash flow dropped from $17.3 million to only $3.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we do get a nice increase from $11.6 million to $18 million. However, EBITDA for the company dropped year over year from $20.4 million to $19.6 million.

Another issue that I have with the company is that management seems to have no idea what financial results will look like even a short time out from the present moment. Previous guidance provided by the company called for revenue of between $455 million and $480 million for the 2024 fiscal year. But that range has since been reduced to between $436 million and $445 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA guidance has been reduced from between $42 million and $48 million to between $39 million and $42 million. If we take the midpoint there and assume that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate, we would get a reading for this year of $28.1 million.

Normally, this kind of volatility would cause me to rate a company a ‘hold’ or lower. But there are two positives that caused me to rate this business a soft ‘buy’. The first is the fact that the enterprise has no debt on its books and enjoys a net cash position of $68.7 million. Stacked up against a market capitalization of only $243.8 million, this is a massive amount of cash to have. And the second is that shares are quite cheap. Using the estimates for 2024 and the historical results for 2023, we can see in the chart above just how cheap shares are. I then compared the company to five similar firms, shown in the table below. On a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than Nature's Sunshine Products is. This drops to one of the five when using the EV to EBITDA scenario.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Nature's Sunshine Products 8.7 4.5
The Beauty Health Company (SKIN) 99.8 40.4
Medifast (MED) 3.2 0.7
Coty Inc (COTY) 13.3 13.6
FitLife Brands (FTLF) 17.5 13.6
Edgewell Personal Care Company (EPC) 8.6 9.0

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I definitely think that Nature's Sunshine Products is an interesting firm. I am encouraged by how cheap shares are, and I like its balance sheet. Management also seems to think that shares are undervalued, as evidenced by the fact that the company has recently repurchased $7.7 million worth of stock and has another $9.9 million of capacity under the share buyback program that's in place. These positives are offset to some extent by the historical volatility of the company, especially at a time when the industry that it operates is growing consistently. Because of that, the best I can rate the company is a soft ‘buy’.
